name: Product Manager — Growth Case
description: PM interview at a consumer SaaS. Heavy on case-style questions and metrics reasoning.

context.candidate: 4 years PM experience, prior background in data analytics, currently at Linear

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Welcome. Tell me about yourself and a product you've shipped recently that you're proud of.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: I'm a senior PM at Linear focused on the growth surface. The thing I'm most proud of recently is we shipped a redesigned onboarding flow that lifted week-1 activation by 18%.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Notion's daily active users are flat quarter-over-quarter. Walk me through how you'd diagnose what's going on.

turns.label: Open-ended diagnostic case — user wants brainstorm to structure approach

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: I'd start by decomposing DAU into new vs returning, and looking at retention curves across cohorts. Flat DAU usually means new acquisition is offsetting churn.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Good. Say you find that week-4 retention has dropped from 35% to 28% in the last six months. What do you do?

turns.label: User wants to refine — sound more decisive

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: First I'd segment by cohort source. If it's specific to organic signups vs paid, that points at marketing or product-market fit drift.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: What feature would you build to address it, assuming you confirm the issue is in-product engagement?

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: I'd hypothesize that core habit-forming actions like creating a doc, sharing it, and getting a comment back are where week-4 retention is decided. So I'd build a habit-loop activation campaign — in-product nudges to invite a teammate within the first 7 days.

turns.label: User wants a concrete metric example added

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: Primary metric: lift in week-4 retention for the treatment cohort. Guardrails: no regression in week-1 activation, no drop in NPS, and we'd watch share rates and comment rates as leading indicators.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Last one — any questions for me?

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: Two things — what does Notion's PM career ladder look like past senior, and how does the growth team interface with the core product team on shared metrics?
